old_claim.note: Maritime Insurance provides a capability that enables this technology without being the only possible path.

new_claim.note: Marine insurance reduced financial risk in ship and cargo ventures, supporting investment and scaling in the long-distance shipping that triangular trade required.

source_shape.source_locator: Marine insurance article: Britannica defines marine insurance as indemnifying ships or cargo against navigation risks and says understanding it is essential to understanding the shipping industry.
source_shape.source_claim_summary: Marine insurance supported investment and risk management for shipping, while triangular trade required repeated Atlantic shipping legs.
source_shape.source_support_rationale: The source supports maritime insurance as commercial risk infrastructure for scaling maritime trade, not as a technical prerequisite for the existence of triangular trade routes.

ontology_before: The old edge treated maritime insurance as a generic enabling capability for triangular trade.
ontology_after: The corrected edge models maritime insurance as commercial risk-management infrastructure for scaling ship and cargo ventures in long-distance Atlantic trade.
invariant_preserved_or_changed: Changed edge type: maritime_insurance is now a commercial_or_scaling_dependency rather than a direct enabling prerequisite.
would_reject_if: The triangular_trade node is later narrowed to a single uninsured voyage pattern rather than a recurring trade system.
A stronger source shows marine insurance was a direct operational requirement for every triangular-trade voyage.
The graph adds a narrower maritime-risk-finance node that should replace maritime_insurance on this edge.
short_reason: Maritime insurance scaled the shipping system by managing risk rather than enabling the route pattern itself.
